name: Game Loop
summary: The heartbeat of every game â€” decouple update rate from render rate. Fixed timestep for physics/gameplay logic, variable timestep for rendering.
when_to_use: Every game needs a game loop. Use fixed timestep when physics determinism matters (multiplayer, physics puzzles). Use variable for pure rendering.
pitfalls: Spiral of death when update time > timestep. Accumulator pattern prevents this.

name: Object Pool
summary: Pre-allocate a fixed set of objects and reuse them instead of creating/destroying. Eliminates GC pressure for frequently spawned objects (bullets, particles, enemies).
when_to_use: Any object spawned and destroyed at high frequency. Rule of thumb: > 10 spawns/second benefits from pooling.
pitfalls: Forgetting to reset state on return. Pool exhaustion if not sized correctly. Don't pool objects that are rarely created.

name: Observer / Event System
summary: Decouple event senders from receivers. One object broadcasts an event; any number of listeners react without the sender knowing who they are.
when_to_use: Achievement systems, UI updates, cross-system communication, audio triggers. Anywhere a 1:N notification is needed.
pitfalls: Memory leaks from unregistered listeners. Event storms (cascading events). Debugging difficulty â€” hard to trace what triggered what.

name: Command Pattern
summary: Encapsulate an action as an object. Enables undo/redo, replay, input buffering, and networked action transmission.
when_to_use: Undo/redo systems. Input buffering for fighting games (frame-perfect inputs). Action replay. Networked games where actions must be serialized.
pitfalls: Overhead for simple games. Command objects accumulate if undo buffer is unbounded.

name: Finite State Machine
summary: Model discrete behavior states and explicit transitions. Character movement (idle/walk/run/jump), enemy AI, UI screens.
when_to_use: Any entity with clearly defined, mutually exclusive behavioral states. The moment you have > 2 if/else chains checking state flags, use a FSM.
pitfalls: State explosion for complex behavior. Transition spaghetti. Prefer Hierarchical FSM (HFSM) or Behavior Trees for complex AI.

name: Entity Component System
summary: Separate data (components) from behavior (systems). Entities are IDs, components are pure data, systems process components. Enables cache-friendly iteration and radical composition.
when_to_use: Large numbers of similar entities (1000+ objects). Games requiring high performance iteration. When OOP inheritance hierarchies become unwieldy.
pitfalls: Conceptual shift from OOP. Harder to reason about single-entity behavior. Unity DOTS requires significant buy-in. Over-engineering for small games.

name: Behavior Tree
summary: Hierarchical task network for game AI. Leaf nodes are actions/conditions; composite nodes (Sequence, Selector, Parallel) control flow. Readable, debuggable, designer-friendly.
when_to_use: NPC AI more complex than a simple FSM. When designers need to author AI without code. Unreal uses BTs natively; Unity needs a plugin.
pitfalls: Harder to express emergent behavior than utility AI. Can become unreadably deep. Tick rate management is non-obvious.

name: Spatial Partition
summary: Divide space to accelerate proximity queries. Quadtrees (2D), octrees (3D), grids. Reduces enemy detection from O(n^2) to O(n log n) or O(n).
when_to_use: Any game with proximity checks (collision, line of sight, area-of-effect). Essential when entity count > ~100.
pitfalls: Update cost when entities move frequently. Cell size tuning. Not worth it for < 50 entities.

name: Dirty Flag
summary: Mark derived data as stale when source data changes. Recompute only when needed. Avoids redundant expensive computations (bounding boxes, pathfinding, shadow maps).
when_to_use: Any derived computation that is expensive and the source changes less often than the derived data is read.
pitfalls: Forgetting to set the flag. Cascading dirty flags. Difficult to debug stale state bugs.

name: Resource Economy
summary: Model resource flows: sources, drains, converters, and pools. Machinations notation. Ensures the economy is balanced and provides meaningful player decisions.
when_to_use: Any game with resources (health, currency, ammo, mana, time). Essential for idle games, city builders, 4X, and RPGs.
pitfalls: Runaway leader problem. Resource starvation killing fun. Ignoring secondary/tertiary resources.

name: Rollback Netcode
summary: Each client predicts game state locally. On mismatch, roll back to last confirmed state and resimulate. Minimizes perceived input latency for fast-paced games.
when_to_use: Fighting games, action games where input latency > 2 frames is noticeable. Requires deterministic simulation.
pitfalls: Determinism requirement is strict â€” any float divergence breaks rollback. Visual artifacts during rollback. High CPU cost.

name: Snapshot Interpolation
summary: Server sends world snapshots at fixed intervals. Clients interpolate between the two most recent snapshots for smooth rendering. No prediction required.
when_to_use: Slower-paced games where 100-200ms of visual latency is acceptable (MOBAs, MMORPGs, strategy games). Simpler than rollback.
pitfalls: Visual lag equal to interpolation buffer. Objects teleport if buffer underruns. Jitter from variable network conditions.

name: Client-Side Prediction
summary: Client immediately applies player input locally. Server authoritative result arrives later â€” client reconciles if diverged. Feels instant to the player.
when_to_use: Any game with player-controlled movement over a network. The minimum viable netcode feature for good game feel.
pitfalls: Reconciliation artifacts (rubber-banding). Complex for non-deterministic physics. Must be combined with server authority to prevent cheating.

name: Level of Detail (LOD)
summary: Swap to lower-fidelity representations at distance. Mesh LOD, shader LOD, animation LOD, AI LOD. Essential for open-world games.
when_to_use: Any game with objects visible at varying distances. GPU-bound performance with many visible objects.
pitfalls: Pop-in artifacts at LOD boundaries. LOD transitions requiring designer tuning. Memory cost of multiple LOD levels.

name: Procedural Content Generation
summary: Algorithms generate game content (levels, items, quests, terrain). Seeded RNG for reproducibility. Enables infinite replayability at lower content creation cost.
when_to_use: Roguelikes and roguelites. Infinite worlds (Minecraft). Games where replayability is core. When manual content creation cost is prohibitive.
pitfalls: Quality floor is hard to guarantee. Player understanding of rules can break immersion. Requires more design constraint work upfront.

name: Save System
summary: Serialize/deserialize complete game state. Patterns: snapshot (save everything), delta (save only changes), checkpoint (save at fixed points).
when_to_use: Every game with persistence. Snapshot for small games. Delta for large games. Checkpoint for games with intentional save friction (roguelikes, soulslikes).
pitfalls: Save file corruption. Version migration when save format changes. Platform-specific save APIs (consoles). Autosave timing causing hitches.

name: Camera System
summary: Spring-arm, look-ahead, dead zones, smoothing, and cinematic cuts. The camera communicates game state and feel â€” often the hardest part of 3D game dev to get right.
when_to_use: Every 3D game. Even 2D games benefit from camera polish (screenshake, follow logic, parallax).
pitfalls: Camera clipping through geometry. Motion sickness in first-person. Forgetting to handle transition between gameplay and cutscene cameras.

name: Input Buffering
summary: Store the last N frames of player input. Allow actions to execute slightly late if conditions aren't met yet. Makes controls feel responsive and forgiving.
when_to_use: Any action game with precise timing windows (combos, jumps, attacks). Adds perceived responsiveness without changing actual game speed.
pitfalls: Buffer window too large feels like input lag. Too small defeats the purpose. Flushing buffer correctly on state transitions.

name: Gameplay Ability System
summary: Data-driven ability framework: abilities, effects, and attributes are configurable assets. Handles cooldowns, costs, stacking, buffs/debuffs, and targeting.
when_to_use: RPGs, MOBAs, action RPGs â€” any game with a complex ability set. Unreal has GAS built-in; Unity requires custom implementation.
pitfalls: High upfront complexity. Over-engineering for simple games. GAS learning curve in Unreal is steep.

name: Audio Manager
summary: Centralized audio playback with priority, ducking, spatial audio, and pool management. Prevents overlapping/competing sounds and manages audio budgets.
when_to_use: Every game with audio. The naive approach (AudioSource.PlayClipAtPoint everywhere) collapses immediately in production.
pitfalls: Audio source pool exhaustion. Forgetting to release pooled sources. Mixing bus complexity. Platform-specific audio codec support.

name: Screen-Space UI Architecture
summary: Separate UI data from UI rendering. Model (data) -> ViewModel (formatted) -> View (rendered). Enables UI skinning, localization, and A/B testing without touching game logic.
when_to_use: Any game with substantial HUD or menus. The direct approach (read game state in UI directly) becomes unmaintainable quickly.
pitfalls: Overhead for simple games. MVVM pattern unfamiliar to game devs coming from Unity approach.

name: Game Feel (Juice)
summary: Layered sensory feedback: screenshake, hitstop, audio cues, particle bursts, controller rumble, animation squash/stretch. Transforms mechanical actions into satisfying moments.
when_to_use: Every game. Add after core mechanics work. The rule: if an action has a consequence, it should have a feel response.
pitfalls: Over-juicing (everything shakes all the time). Juice masking bad game feel instead of fixing it. Accessibility concerns (photosensitivity, motion sickness).

name: Dialog System
summary: Data-driven branching dialog with conditions and consequences. Separate dialog data (Yarn, Ink, JSON) from display logic. Support localization from day one.
when_to_use: Any game with NPC conversations, narrative choices, or voiced dialogue. Build data-driven from the start â€” retrofitting is painful.
pitfalls: Ignoring localization until too late. Dialog conditions tightly coupled to game state. No way for writers to preview without running the game.

name: Achievement System
summary: Observer-based: gameplay events -> achievement evaluator -> unlock -> platform API. Decouple achievement logic completely from gameplay systems.
when_to_use: Any shipped game on Steam, console, or mobile. Design achievements as a secondary design layer that rewards exploration.
pitfalls: Coupling achievement checks to gameplay code. Missing platform API rate limits. Retroactive achievement unlocks when adding late.

name: Game Manager / Service Locator
summary: Central registry for global services (AudioManager, SaveSystem, EventBus). Avoids singleton abuse while providing global access to cross-cutting concerns.
when_to_use: When multiple systems need access to shared services and direct dependency injection becomes unwieldy.
pitfalls: Becomes a grab-bag of unrelated functionality. Hidden dependencies. Prefer DI for testable systems. Don't put game logic here.

name: Dead Reckoning
summary: Extrapolate object position between network updates using velocity/acceleration. Client continues moving an entity in the predicted direction until the next authoritative position arrives.
when_to_use: Any networked game with moving entities where updates arrive slower than frame rate. Racing, flight sims, FPS.
pitfalls: Visible snapping when prediction diverges from actual. Requires smooth correction blending. Doesn't work well for erratic movement.

name: Area of Interest
summary: Server sends only state changes within each player's visibility radius. Massively reduces bandwidth for large worlds. Essential for MMOs and battle royale games.
when_to_use: Large multiplayer worlds where each player sees only a fraction of total state. MMOs, battle royale, open-world multiplayer.
pitfalls: Popping when entities enter/leave radius. Edge cases at boundary. Dynamic radius sizing for different entity types.

name: Delta Compression
summary: Send only changed fields between snapshots, not full state. Compare snapshot N against snapshot N-1; transmit only diffs. Standard in Unreal Engine replication.
when_to_use: Any networked game with large entity state. Reduces bandwidth proportional to change rate. Most effective for slowly-changing state.
pitfalls: Must handle baseline reset on packet loss. Complexity of diffing nested structs. Bit-packing required for real savings.

name: Deterministic Lockstep
summary: All clients advance simulation by one tick simultaneously. Only inputs are transmitted. Zero bandwidth for state -- all derived from identical simulation. Classic RTS approach.
when_to_use: Games with many units where transmitting all state is impractical. RTS, fighting games, turn-based with simultaneous resolution.
pitfalls: Requires fully deterministic simulation (no floats, fixed-point math). One slow client stalls everyone. Cheating harder to prevent.

name: Lag Compensation
summary: Server rewinds game state to the time of the client's shot before evaluating hit detection. Ensures players hit where they aimed, accounting for their network latency.
when_to_use: Any shooter or action game with projectile/hitscan weapons. Essential for fair hit detection in high-latency environments.
pitfalls: Getting shot behind cover (server rewound past your dodge). Maximum rewind window needed. CPU cost of storing history buffers.

name: Draw Call Batching
summary: Combine multiple mesh draw calls into one by merging geometry or using GPU instancing. Each draw call has CPU overhead -- reducing count directly improves frame rate.
when_to_use: When CPU-bound on rendering. Scenes with many small objects sharing materials. UI rendering. Particle systems.
pitfalls: Dynamic batching can cost more CPU than it saves. Material breaks prevent batching. Overdraw from merged geometry.

name: Occlusion Culling
summary: Skip rendering objects hidden behind other geometry. CPU determines visibility before GPU renders. Unreal uses Hierarchical ZBuffer; Unity has dynamic occlusion baking.
when_to_use: Dense indoor environments, cities, any scene with significant overdraw. Most effective with many opaque occluders.
pitfalls: CPU cost of occlusion queries can exceed GPU savings in open scenes. Baked occlusion data becomes stale. Transparent objects can't occlude.

name: Asset Streaming
summary: Load and unload assets dynamically based on player position. Prevents loading entire world into memory. UE5 World Partition, Unity Addressables, Godot scenes.
when_to_use: Open-world games, large levels, games targeting consoles or mobile with limited RAM. Any game larger than available memory.
pitfalls: Hitching during async loads. Priority system needed for visible vs. predicted assets. Memory fragmentation over time.

name: Job System / Task Parallelism
summary: Dispatch CPU work as data-parallel jobs across worker threads. Unity Job System, Unreal Task Graph. Essential for scalable performance on multi-core hardware.
when_to_use: CPU-bound workloads: physics, AI ticking, pathfinding, procedural generation. Any work that can be split into independent chunks.
pitfalls: Race conditions and data dependencies. Job overhead for trivially small tasks. Debugging parallel code is harder.

name: GPU Skinning
summary: Move skeletal animation vertex transforms from CPU to GPU. Eliminates CPU bottleneck for games with many animated characters. Standard in modern engines.
when_to_use: Games with many animated characters on screen. Crowd scenes, battle games, MMOs with visible player characters.
pitfalls: Requires compute shader support. Blend shape combinations add complexity. CPU still needed for root motion and IK.

name: Utility AI
summary: Score all available actions by their utility to the current situation. Highest-scoring action wins. More flexible than FSMs, simpler to tune than GOAP. The Sims uses this approach.
when_to_use: NPCs with many possible actions and nuanced priorities. Life sims, strategy AI, companion AI. When behavior should feel emergent.
pitfalls: Utility curves need extensive tuning. Hard to debug why an action was chosen. Can feel robotic without randomization.

name: Goal-Oriented Action Planning (GOAP)
summary: AI determines a sequence of actions to satisfy a goal state using forward planning. F.E.A.R.'s AI is the classic example. More autonomous than behavior trees.
when_to_use: Complex AI agents that need to chain multiple actions to achieve goals. Stealth games, tactical AI, sandbox worlds.
pitfalls: Planning is expensive -- cache plans. Action precondition/effect design is tricky. Hard to guarantee plan validity in dynamic worlds.

name: Steering Behaviors
summary: Combine simple movement forces (seek, flee, arrive, pursue, flock) to produce emergent agent motion. Craig Reynolds' original formulation. Foundation of most game movement AI.
when_to_use: Any AI that needs to move through space. Enemies, crowds, flocking birds, traffic. Combine with NavMesh for obstacle avoidance.
pitfalls: Oscillation when forces conflict. Needs priority or weight tuning. Local minima in force combinations.

name: Navigation Mesh (NavMesh)
summary: Pre-bake walkable surface polygons for pathfinding. A* finds paths on the mesh. Dynamic obstacles handled by local avoidance (RVO/ORCA). Engine-native in UE5, Unity, Godot.
when_to_use: Any game with AI agents that move through 3D space. Most games use NavMesh for ground navigation.
pitfalls: Bake time for large worlds. Dynamic NavMesh updates are expensive. Flying/swimming AI needs different approaches.

name: Perception System
summary: Model how AI agents sense the world: sight (line-of-sight, field of view), hearing (radius, volume), and smell. Separate perception from reaction for modular AI design.
when_to_use: Stealth games, tactical shooters, horror games. Any game where AI awareness of the player matters for gameplay.
pitfalls: Performance cost of sight checks (raycasts). Tuning perception thresholds for fun vs. realism. Memory of past stimuli adds complexity.

name: Service Locator
summary: Provide a global access point to a service (audio, physics, logging) without coupling to the concrete implementation. A controlled alternative to raw singletons â€” swap implementations for testing or platform differences.
when_to_use: Cross-cutting services needed everywhere (audio, analytics, save system). When you want to swap implementations at runtime (mock audio in tests, null logger in production).
pitfalls: Still a global â€” hides dependencies. Overuse leads to the same problems as singletons. Prefer constructor injection when feasible. Unity's FindObjectOfType is the worst form of this.

name: Event Queue
summary: Buffer events and process them later in a controlled order. Decouples the moment something happens from the moment it is handled. Prevents cascading side effects during update loops.
when_to_use: Audio event dispatch, UI notification systems, networked event replay, undo/redo systems. Whenever processing order matters or you need to defer work.
pitfalls: Queue growth under load â€” needs a cap or drain policy. Debugging deferred events is harder than synchronous calls. Stale references if objects are destroyed before their events process.

name: Double Buffer
summary: Maintain two copies of state â€” one being read, one being written. Swap atomically when the write is complete. Prevents tearing and ordering bugs during concurrent reads and writes.
when_to_use: Physics state updates (read current, write next), AI world model snapshots, render state for multithreaded rendering, network snapshot interpolation.
pitfalls: Doubles memory usage. Swap timing is critical â€” a missed swap causes a stale frame. Not always needed if you can guarantee update order.

name: Type Object
summary: Define entity types as data rather than classes. Each instance holds a reference to a shared type object that defines its stats, behavior, and appearance. Avoids class explosion for content-heavy games.
when_to_use: RPG item/enemy types, card game card definitions, any system with hundreds of entity variants. Unity ScriptableObjects and Unreal DataAssets are engine implementations of this pattern.
pitfalls: Type changes at runtime can be confusing. Inheritance between type objects adds complexity. Need a robust editor for designers.

name: Subclass Sandbox
summary: Define a base class with a set of protected operations. Subclasses implement behavior by combining those operations â€” never accessing external systems directly. Limits coupling surface.
when_to_use: Spell/ability systems where each spell is a subclass. Unity MonoBehaviour is a sandbox â€” Start, Update, GetComponent are the provided operations. Keeps subclass authors safe.
pitfalls: Base class grows over time as subclasses need more operations. Tight coupling to the sandbox API. Consider composition if the base class exceeds 15-20 operations.

name: Update Method
summary: Give each active entity an Update() method called once per frame. The game loop iterates a list of active entities and ticks each one. Simple, universal, and the default in most engines.
when_to_use: Default for entity behavior in Unity (MonoBehaviour.Update), Godot (_process), Unreal (Tick). Good starting point before optimizing to ECS or batch processing.
pitfalls: Per-entity virtual dispatch is cache-unfriendly at scale. Call overhead adds up with thousands of entities. Profile before assuming it's a bottleneck â€” it's often fine.

name: Component (OOP)
summary: Attach reusable behavior components to entities instead of using deep inheritance hierarchies. Each component handles one concern (physics, rendering, AI). Compose entities from components at runtime.
when_to_use: When entity types share some behaviors but not others (e.g., some enemies fly, some swim). Unity's GameObject+Component, Godot's Node system, Unreal's ActorComponent are all this pattern.
pitfalls: Inter-component communication can be awkward (GetComponent lookups). Component ordering may matter (physics before rendering). Not the same as ECS â€” this is the OOP variant.

name: Flyweight
summary: Share intrinsic (immutable) state across many instances while keeping extrinsic (per-instance) state separate. Reduces memory usage when thousands of objects share the same mesh, texture, or stats.
when_to_use: Instanced rendering (thousands of trees sharing one mesh), tile maps, particle systems. GPU instancing in Unity/Unreal is hardware-level flyweight.
pitfalls: Adds indirection. Mutable shared state causes bugs. Must clearly separate intrinsic from extrinsic state at design time.

name: Prototype
summary: Create new objects by cloning an existing prototype instead of constructing from scratch. Useful when setup is expensive or configuration-heavy. Engine spawn functions are implementations of this pattern.
when_to_use: Prefab instantiation (Unity Instantiate, Godot duplicate), entity factories, level editors with copy-paste. When objects need complex initialization that a constructor alone can't express.
pitfalls: Deep vs. shallow clone confusion â€” nested references may share state unexpectedly. Clone must properly reset runtime state (health, position). Prototype bloat if prototypes accumulate.

name: Bytecode / Script Object
summary: Encode behavior as data-driven instructions that a virtual machine interprets at runtime. Enables designers to create complex behavior without recompiling. Visual scripting and dialogue systems use this pattern.
when_to_use: Modding support, visual scripting (Unreal Blueprints, Godot VisualScript), dialogue trees (Ink, Yarn Spinner), AI behavior configuration for designers.
pitfalls: Debugging interpreted code is harder. Performance ceiling vs. native code. Version compatibility of saved bytecode across patches. Tooling investment is high.

name: Rollback Netcode (GGPO-style)
summary: Predict local input and simulate ahead, then rollback and resimulate if remote inputs differ. Provides zero-latency local feel for fast-paced games.
when_to_use: Fighting games, fast-paced action, any game where input latency is unacceptable. Best when state snapshots are small and simulation is deterministic.
pitfalls: Determinism required across all clients. Large state snapshots expensive. Rollback frames cause CPU spikes. Visual artifacts during corrections.

name: Snapshot Interpolation
summary: Buffer received server snapshots and interpolate between them for smooth rendering. Trades visual latency for smoothness.
when_to_use: Games tolerant of 2-3 frame visual delay. MMOs, cooperative games, any game where slight visual lag is acceptable for smooth motion.
pitfalls: Adds inherent visual latency equal to interpolation buffer. Extrapolation needed at buffer edges. Jitter when packets arrive unevenly.

name: Client-Side Prediction
summary: Apply player input locally before server confirmation, then reconcile when server state arrives. Makes the game feel responsive despite network latency.
when_to_use: FPS, action games where the player must feel immediate response. Movement prediction is the most common use case.
pitfalls: Mispredictions cause visible correction snaps. Must store input history for reconciliation. Server must validate all predicted actions.

name: Lag Compensation
summary: Server rewinds game state to the time a client fired, checking hits against historical positions. Ensures shooters feel fair despite latency.
when_to_use: Hitscan weapons, projectile games, any competitive game where hit registration must account for player latency.
pitfalls: Server rewind is CPU-expensive. Requires reliable timestamp synchronization. Players with high latency can hit around corners (peeker's advantage).

name: Authoritative Server
summary: Server is the single source of truth for game state. Clients send inputs, server validates and broadcasts results. Prevents most forms of cheating.
when_to_use: All competitive multiplayer games. Essential for ranked play, esports, any game where cheating must be prevented.
pitfalls: Adds round-trip latency to every action. Must handle prediction and correction smoothly. Server costs scale with player count.

name: Deterministic Lockstep
summary: All clients simulate the same deterministic game state from identical inputs. Only inputs are transmitted, not state. Extremely bandwidth-efficient.
when_to_use: RTS games, turn-based games, physics-heavy games like Rocket League. Any game where full state sync would be too expensive.
pitfalls: All clients must be perfectly deterministic (floating point, iteration order). Desyncs are fatal and hard to debug. Input delay equals round-trip time.

name: Dead Reckoning
summary: Predict entity positions based on last known velocity and direction. Correct when actual updates arrive. Reduces bandwidth for slowly-changing entities.
when_to_use: MMOs, large world games with many entities. Vehicles, NPCs, and distant players whose exact position matters less.
pitfalls: Accumulates error over time. Correction snaps are visible. Needs threshold tuning per entity type.

name: Entity Interpolation
summary: Smoothly interpolate remote entity positions between received network updates. Renders other players and AI at a slight visual delay for smooth motion.
when_to_use: Third-party entities (other players, AI) in any networked game. Essential complement to client-side prediction.
pitfalls: Inherent 2-frame visual delay. Must handle teleportation and large corrections gracefully. Buffer underrun causes freezing.

name: Interest Management (Area of Interest)
summary: Only send network updates for entities within a player's area of interest. Reduces bandwidth and server load for large-world games.
when_to_use: MMOs, battle royale, open world multiplayer. Any game with more entities than a single client can process.
pitfalls: AOI boundary artifacts (entities popping in/out). Load balancing when many players cluster. Spatial data structure overhead.

name: Tick Rate Management
summary: Control how frequently the server simulates and sends updates. Balance between accuracy (high tick rate) and bandwidth/CPU cost (low tick rate).
when_to_use: All online games. Choose tick rate based on game speed: 128Hz for competitive FPS, 20-30Hz for MMOs, 60Hz for action games.
pitfalls: Low tick rate causes inaccurate physics and missed collisions. High tick rate burns bandwidth and CPU. Client interpolation must match.

name: Delta Compression
summary: Send only the differences between consecutive state snapshots instead of full state. Dramatically reduces bandwidth for games with large state.
when_to_use: Bandwidth-constrained games, mobile multiplayer, any game with large world state that changes incrementally.
pitfalls: Complex state tracking. Baselines must be acknowledged before sending deltas. Lost baseline packets require full resync.

name: Reliable UDP (RUDP)
summary: Build reliability and ordering on top of UDP to get TCP-like guarantees without TCP's head-of-line blocking. Used by most game networking libraries.
when_to_use: Games needing ordered delivery without TCP latency. Essentially all real-time multiplayer games use some form of RUDP.
pitfalls: Re-implementation complexity is high. RTT measurement and congestion control needed. Libraries like ENet, LiteNetLib exist for this.

name: Lobby / Session Management
summary: Manage the lifecycle of multiplayer sessions: matchmaking, lobby creation, player join/leave, game start, and post-game cleanup.
when_to_use: Any multiplayer game with discrete matches or sessions. Needed for matchmaking, party systems, and server browser.
pitfalls: State machine complexity. Graceful host migration in P2P. Handling disconnects, timeouts, and rejoins during lobby phase.

name: NAT Traversal (STUN/TURN/ICE)
summary: Punch through NAT firewalls to establish direct peer-to-peer connections. Uses STUN for hole-punching, TURN as relay fallback, ICE for negotiation.
when_to_use: P2P multiplayer games, voice chat, any direct client-to-client communication. Required when players are behind home routers.
pitfalls: TURN relay cost when STUN fails (symmetric NAT). Success rate varies by NAT type. TURN servers add latency and hosting cost.

name: Relay Server
summary: Route all player traffic through a server relay when direct P2P fails. Guarantees connectivity at the cost of added latency.
when_to_use: When direct P2P fails due to NAT issues. As fallback for STUN/TURN. For small-scale games where dedicated servers are too expensive.
pitfalls: Relay adds latency (extra hop). Server bandwidth costs scale with player count. Must handle relay server failover.

name: Connection State Machine
summary: Model the network connection lifecycle as a state machine: disconnected, connecting, handshake, connected, reconnecting, disconnecting.
when_to_use: All online games. Provides clean handling of connection edge cases, timeout recovery, and graceful degradation.
pitfalls: Edge cases: timeout during handshake, reconnect during disconnect, version mismatch. Must handle all state transitions cleanly.

name: Bandwidth Budget
summary: Allocate a fixed bandwidth budget per tick and prioritize which entities and properties get sent within that budget.
when_to_use: Mobile multiplayer, MMOs, any game targeting bandwidth-constrained connections. Essential for games with many networked entities.
pitfalls: Oversending causes mobile churn and packet loss. Complex to tune per-entity priority. Starvation of low-priority entities.

name: Replication Priority
summary: Assign priority scores to networked entities based on relevance (distance, visibility, gameplay importance) to decide update frequency.
when_to_use: Open world multiplayer, large server rooms. When bandwidth budget forces choices about what to replicate.
pitfalls: Priority starvation if not balanced. Nearby enemies must always be high-priority. Priority recalculation has CPU cost.

name: Reconnect / Rejoin
summary: Allow players to reconnect to an in-progress game after a disconnect. Restore their state, position, and inventory seamlessly.
when_to_use: Session-based competitive games (MOBAs, battle royale). Any game where disconnects shouldn't mean permanent loss.
pitfalls: State restore complexity. Anti-cheat implications of reconnection. Timeout window tuning. Handling the player's absence during disconnect.

name: Time Synchronization (NTP-style)
summary: Synchronize clocks between server and clients using round-trip time measurement. Essential for lag compensation and physics sync.
when_to_use: Any game using lag compensation, physics sync, or time-based gameplay mechanics. Foundation for client-side prediction.
pitfalls: Clock drift between updates. Asymmetric latency complicates RTT calculation. Must filter outlier measurements.

name: GPU Instancing
summary: Render many copies of the same mesh in a single draw call by sending per-instance data (transforms, colors) to the GPU. Massive performance win for vegetation, crowds, particles.
when_to_use: 100+ identical meshes (grass, trees, rocks, crowd NPCs, bullets). Any scene with repeated geometry.
pitfalls: Broken by per-instance material property blocks in some engines. All instances must share the same mesh and material. LOD interaction requires care.

name: Draw Call Batching (Static)
summary: Combine multiple static meshes into a single draw call by merging their geometry. Reduces CPU overhead from draw call submission.
when_to_use: Static geometry, UI elements, any objects that don't move. Most effective when many small objects share the same material.
pitfalls: Moving objects break batches. Increases memory usage from combined mesh. Overdraw may increase. Only works with shared materials.

name: LOD Management (Level of Detail)
summary: Switch between mesh complexity levels based on camera distance. Distant objects use fewer triangles, saving GPU fill rate and vertex processing.
when_to_use: Open world, any 3D game with draw distance. Essential for maintaining frame rate with detailed environments.
pitfalls: Pop-in artifacts if LOD thresholds not tuned. Requires authoring multiple LOD meshes. Cross-fade transitions have GPU cost.

name: Frustum Culling
summary: Skip rendering objects outside the camera's view frustum. The most fundamental culling technique â€” most engines do this automatically.
when_to_use: Any 3D scene. Typically engine-provided but understanding it helps when debugging performance.
pitfalls: CPU cost of frustum tests for many objects. Must account for shadow-casting objects outside the view. Bounding volume accuracy matters.

name: Occlusion Culling
summary: Skip rendering objects hidden behind other objects. Uses precomputed visibility data or runtime GPU queries to determine occlusion.
when_to_use: Dense indoor environments, urban scenes. Any scene where many objects are hidden behind walls or large occluders.
pitfalls: Baking time for precomputed occlusion. Dynamic scenes require runtime GPU occlusion queries. False positives cause pop-in.

name: Frame Budget Management
summary: Allocate a fixed time budget per frame (e.g. 16.6ms for 60fps) and distribute it across subsystems. Monitor and enforce budgets in real-time.
when_to_use: All real-time games targeting a consistent frame rate. Essential for console certification and VR (where dropped frames cause nausea).
pitfalls: Budget overruns still happen under load. Profiling required to identify bottlenecks. Dynamic content makes static budgets unreliable.

name: Object Pool (Memory-Focused)
summary: Eliminate runtime allocations in hot paths by pre-allocating and reusing memory blocks. Critical for GC-heavy languages like C# in Unity.
when_to_use: Frequent alloc/dealloc hot paths. Unity IL2CPP/Mono, any C# game code path called per-frame. Particle systems, physics callbacks.
pitfalls: Pool exhaustion under load. Too-large pools waste RAM. Must reset all state on return to pool. Thread safety if pooled across threads.

name: Async Scene Loading
summary: Load scenes and assets in the background while the game continues running. Eliminates loading screens for seamless world transitions.
when_to_use: Open world games, hub-and-spoke level design. Any game where loading screens break immersion.
pitfalls: Loading seams at streaming boundaries. Memory spikes during transition. Must handle partially-loaded state gracefully.

name: Shader Permutation Management
summary: Control shader variant explosion by managing feature toggles, keyword combinations, and compile-time branching in shader code.
when_to_use: Complex rendering pipelines with many material features. When shader compile times or runtime variant switching becomes a bottleneck.
pitfalls: Permutation explosion (2^N variants). Long cook/compile times. Runtime variant loading causes hitches. Memory cost of compiled shaders.

name: Texture Streaming
summary: Load texture mip levels on demand based on camera distance. Keeps GPU memory usage bounded while supporting large texture budgets.
when_to_use: Open world games with large texture budgets. Any game targeting platforms with limited VRAM.
pitfalls: Texture pop-in as mips load. Streaming distance tuning per asset. I/O bandwidth contention with other loading.

name: Asset Bundle / Addressables
summary: Package assets into loadable bundles for DLC, patching, and modular content. Unity Addressables and Unreal Pak files enable on-demand loading.
when_to_use: DLC content, modular games, live-service titles. Any game that needs to update content without full rebuilds.
pitfalls: Dependency cycles between bundles. Bundle granularity tuning (too fine = overhead, too coarse = waste). Version compatibility.

name: CPU Job Scheduling (Job System)
summary: Distribute work across CPU cores using a job/task system. Enables parallel physics, AI, and simulation without manual thread management.
when_to_use: CPU-bound workloads: physics simulation, AI pathfinding, procedural generation. Unity DOTS, Unreal TaskGraph, Bevy ECS.
pitfalls: Race conditions if jobs share mutable state. Job dependency graph complexity. Debugging parallel code is harder. Overhead for small jobs.

name: ECS Chunk / Archetype Layout
summary: Store entity components in contiguous memory chunks grouped by archetype (component combination). Enables cache-friendly iteration over entities.
when_to_use: ECS-based games using Unity DOTS, Bevy, or custom ECS. When iterating over thousands of entities per frame.
pitfalls: Archetype fragmentation from unique component combinations. Structural changes (add/remove component) are costly. Memory waste from sparse archetypes.

name: SIMD-Friendly Data Layout
summary: Arrange data in Structure-of-Arrays (SoA) layout for SIMD vectorization. Process 4-8 entities per instruction in physics and math-heavy code.
when_to_use: High-frequency simulation loops, particle systems, physics solvers. When profiling shows ALU-bound bottlenecks.
pitfalls: Platform portability of SIMD intrinsics. Alignment requirements. Code readability suffers. Not all algorithms vectorize cleanly.

name: GC-Free Hot Path
summary: Eliminate garbage collection allocations from per-frame code paths. Use structs, pre-allocated buffers, and object pools to avoid GC spikes.
when_to_use: Unity IL2CPP and Mono, any C# game code. Frame-rate-sensitive code paths: Update(), FixedUpdate(), render callbacks.
pitfalls: Requires discipline across entire API surface. Hard to audit for hidden allocations (string concat, LINQ, boxing). Unity-specific APIs have GC traps.

name: Handle-Based Resource Management
summary: Reference resources by opaque handles (index + generation) instead of pointers. Enables safe resource reuse, defragmentation, and dangling-reference detection.
when_to_use: Custom engines, Rust game development. Any system managing a large pool of resources with dynamic lifetimes.
pitfalls: Handle recycling risks ABA problem without generation counters. Indirection cost for every access. API ergonomics vs raw pointers.

name: World Streaming (Terrain)
summary: Stream terrain chunks and world data in and out of memory based on player position. Enables infinite world sizes with bounded memory.
when_to_use: Open world games with large terrains. Procedurally generated worlds. Any game where the full world doesn't fit in memory.
pitfalls: Seam artifacts between terrain chunks. Server authority at streaming boundaries in multiplayer. I/O latency causes pop-in.

name: Level Streaming Triggers
summary: Use trigger volumes or distance thresholds to load and unload level sections. Provides designer control over streaming behavior.
when_to_use: Any large level architecture. Linear games with hub areas. Buildings, dungeons, or zones that should load on approach.
pitfalls: Trigger placement sensitivity. Streaming cost spikes when multiple triggers fire simultaneously. Must handle rapid trigger toggling.

name: Preloading / Warm-Up
summary: Load anticipated assets before they're needed. Warm shader caches, instantiate pools, and prepare audio during loading screens or quiet moments.
when_to_use: Games with hard loading requirements (VR, console certification). Cutscene-to-gameplay transitions. Arena/round-based games.
pitfalls: Increased startup time. Memory commitment for preloaded assets that may not be used. Over-preloading wastes resources.

name: Render Thread Separation
summary: Run render command submission on a separate thread from gameplay simulation. Allows the CPU to prepare the next frame while the GPU renders the current one.
when_to_use: CPU-bound render submission scenarios. High draw-call-count scenes. Most modern engines do this automatically.
pitfalls: Synchronization overhead between threads. Command buffer allocation costs. Adds one frame of latency to input response.

name: ECS Entity Lifecycle
summary: Manage entity creation, component addition/removal, and destruction in an ECS framework. Handle deferred operations to avoid iterator invalidation.
when_to_use: Any ECS-based game. Critical for dynamic spawning (bullets, effects) and entity state transitions (alive to dead).
pitfalls: Structural changes during iteration cause undefined behavior. Deferred command buffers add complexity. Entity ID recycling needs care.

name: Reactive Property
summary: Properties that automatically notify observers when their value changes. UI data binding, network sync, and undo/redo benefit from this pattern.
when_to_use: UI data binding (HP bars, score displays). Network property replication. Any system that reacts to state changes.
pitfalls: Cascade of notifications from interdependent properties. Performance overhead per property change. Memory cost for observer lists.

name: Animation State Machine (Extended)
summary: Complex animation blending using layered state machines, blend trees, and animation montages. Handles locomotion, combat, and facial animation simultaneously.
when_to_use: Character-driven games with complex animation requirements. Third-person action, fighting games, cinematic games.
pitfalls: State explosion with many animation combinations. Transition tuning is time-consuming. Blend tree performance cost at many leaves.

name: Physics Layer Matrix
summary: Configure which physics layers can collide with each other using a matrix. Prevents unnecessary collision checks and enables gameplay-specific collision rules.
when_to_use: Any game with physics. Essential for separating player bullets from friendly NPCs, triggers from physics objects, etc.
pitfalls: Layer count limits (32 in most engines). Forgetting to update matrix when adding new layers. Performance impact of too many active collision pairs.

name: Asset Dependency Graph
summary: Track dependencies between assets to enable incremental builds, lazy loading, and impact analysis. Know what breaks when an asset changes.
when_to_use: Large projects with many assets. Pipeline automation. When build times need optimization through incremental cooking.
pitfalls: Circular dependencies. Stale dependency data after manual file moves. Graph traversal cost for deeply nested dependencies.

name: Singleton Service (Anti-Pattern Awareness)
summary: Global access to a single service instance. Common in games (AudioManager, GameManager) but creates tight coupling and testing difficulty.
when_to_use: When exactly one instance is needed and dependency injection is too complex. Prefer Service Locator or DI when possible.
pitfalls: Hidden dependencies. Testing difficulty (global state). Initialization order problems. Thread safety. Often overused in game code.

name: Flyweight (Extended for Games)
summary: Share immutable data across many instances to save memory. In games: shared mesh data, shared stat templates, shared animation rigs.
when_to_use: Many entities sharing base configuration: enemy stat templates, weapon definitions, terrain tile types, particle effect presets.
pitfalls: Mutable state must be per-instance. Thread-safe access to shared data. Cache invalidation when shared data updates.

name: Composite AI
summary: Combine multiple AI techniques (FSM + behavior tree + steering) into layered decision-making. High-level strategy drives low-level behaviors.
when_to_use: Complex NPCs with strategic and tactical behavior. Boss fights, companion AI, RTS unit AI with formation + pathfinding + combat.
pitfalls: Layer interaction complexity. Debugging multi-layer decisions. Priority conflicts between layers.

name: Utility AI
summary: Score all possible actions using utility functions and pick the highest-scoring one. Produces emergent, context-sensitive behavior without explicit state transitions.
when_to_use: NPCs that need nuanced, context-aware decisions. The Sims-style needs system. When behavior trees become too complex.
pitfalls: Tuning utility curves is unintuitive. Hard to guarantee specific behaviors. Debugging why an action was chosen requires logging scores.

name: Planning (GOAP Intro)
summary: Goal-Oriented Action Planning: AI defines goals and searches for action sequences that achieve them. Produces adaptive behavior that finds novel solutions.
when_to_use: Complex AI that needs to adapt to world state. F.E.A.R.-style tactical AI. When hand-authored behavior trees can't cover all situations.
pitfalls: Search cost for long plans. Action precondition/effect modeling is labor-intensive. Plans can produce unexpected or suboptimal behavior.

name: Finite State Machine (AI)
summary: State machine for NPC behavior with states like patrol, alert, attack, flee. Each state defines transitions based on world conditions.
when_to_use: Simple NPC behaviors with fewer than 10 states. Guard AI, door logic, simple enemy behaviors.
pitfalls: State explosion beyond ~10 states makes maintenance impossible. Transition logic becomes a tangled web. Use behavior trees instead for complex AI.

name: Hierarchical Task Network (HTN)
summary: Decompose high-level tasks into primitive actions through methods. Plans are generated by recursively expanding compound tasks.
when_to_use: Strategic AI that needs multi-step plans. RTS base building, resource gathering sequences, complex quest NPCs.
pitfalls: Planning horizon limits. Determinism is brittle in physics simulations. Method authoring requires domain expertise.

name: Blackboard (AI)
summary: Shared memory store for behavior tree nodes and AI subsystems. Decouples perception, decision-making, and action execution.
when_to_use: Behavior tree implementations. Multi-agent coordination. When AI subsystems need to share data without direct coupling.
pitfalls: Stale data if not cleared on state changes. Threading issues in parallel behavior trees. Key naming collisions.

name: Pathfinding (A*)
summary: A* search algorithm for optimal grid/graph path finding. Uses heuristic to guide search toward the goal efficiently.
when_to_use: Grid-based or graph-based navigation. Tile-based games, dungeon crawlers, turn-based strategy movement.
pitfalls: Performance degrades on large open worlds without hierarchical decomposition. Needs nav mesh for 3D environments. Memory usage with large open lists.

name: Flow Field Pathfinding
summary: Generate a vector field over a grid where each cell points toward the destination. All agents sample the field for direction at O(1) per agent per frame.
when_to_use: RTS games with hundreds of units moving to the same destination. Crowd navigation. When per-agent A* is too expensive.
pitfalls: Memory cost scales with grid resolution. Only suitable for shared-destination scenarios. Dynamic obstacles require field regeneration.

name: Memory / Working Memory (AI)
summary: Store perceived facts with timestamps and decay. AI remembers where it last saw the player, heard a sound, or found a resource.
when_to_use: Stealth games where AI should search last known position. NPCs that remember player actions. Realistic AI perception.
pitfalls: Decay timing complexity. Performance with hundreds of agents each maintaining memory stores. Memory serialization for save/load.

name: Squad AI / Group Tactics
summary: Coordinate multiple AI agents toward tactical goals. Assign roles (flanker, suppressor, medic), share targets, execute formations.
when_to_use: Military shooters, tactical RPGs, RTS squad control. When individual AI looks dumb without coordination.
pitfalls: Emergent chaotic behavior when plans conflict. Role assignment needs fallback logic. Debugging group decisions is hard.

name: Cover System
summary: Find and evaluate cover positions based on threat direction, exposure angles, distance, and path safety. AI selects optimal cover dynamically.
when_to_use: Tactical shooters, stealth games, any combat where AI should use environment defensively.
pitfalls: Expensive spatial queries for cover evaluation. Dynamic geometry invalidation. Cover positions can become traps.

name: Threat Assessment
summary: Rank targets by danger using factors like distance, damage output, health, line of sight, and recent actions. AI prioritizes threats dynamically.
when_to_use: Combat AI target selection. Tower defense priority systems. Any AI that must choose between multiple threats.
pitfalls: Reassessment frequency vs CPU cost tradeoff. Hysteresis needed to prevent target flip-flopping. Weight tuning requires playtesting.

name: Patrol Graph
summary: Waypoint-based patrol with variation. AI follows graph edges between patrol points with optional random delays, look-around events, and path alternatives.
when_to_use: Guard patrols in stealth games. NPC daily routines. Area coverage patterns for security AI.
pitfalls: Predictable patterns without variation. Graph authoring is labor-intensive for large levels. Dynamic obstacle handling.

name: Influence Map
summary: Grid overlay where each cell stores a score representing AI interest, danger, or control. Updated per-frame from entity positions and events.
when_to_use: RTS territorial control visualization. Tactical AI positioning. Dynamic difficulty zones. Heat maps for AI decision-making.
pitfalls: Resolution vs performance tradeoff. Update frequency cost with many sources. Propagation delay creates stale data.

name: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)
summary: Build a game tree by random playouts, using UCB1 to balance exploration and exploitation. Used in board games and strategic decisions.
when_to_use: Board game AI (Go, Chess). Strategic decision-making with large branching factors. Card game AI.
pitfalls: Compute budget must be tuned per frame budget. UCB exploration constant needs balancing. Poor with real-time constraints.

name: Minimax + Alpha-Beta Pruning
summary: Adversarial game tree search where one player maximizes score and the other minimizes. Alpha-beta pruning eliminates branches that cannot affect the result.
when_to_use: Two-player perfect-information games (chess, checkers, tic-tac-toe). Turn-based strategy AI.
pitfalls: Depth limit needed for real-time games. Evaluation function quality is critical. Exponential branching without good pruning.

name: Fuzzy Logic AI
summary: Use degree-of-truth rules instead of binary conditions. Somewhat close and very dangerous produce smoother, more natural AI decisions.
when_to_use: Nuanced NPC reactions. Difficulty scaling. When crisp boolean logic produces jarring behavior transitions.
pitfalls: Rule explosion with many input variables. Harder to debug than crisp logic. Defuzzification method affects behavior feel.

name: Crowd Simulation (RVO)
summary: Reciprocal Velocity Obstacles for dense crowd movement. Each agent computes velocity adjustments to avoid all neighbors simultaneously.
when_to_use: Dense crowd scenes (markets, stadiums, evacuation). When navmesh avoidance is not enough for agent-to-agent collision.
pitfalls: Oscillation at high density. Requires velocity damping. Performance scales with agent count squared without spatial acceleration.

name: Emotion / Personality Model
summary: Parameterized NPC personality using models like Big Five or PAD (Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance). Emotions drift based on events and decay over time.
when_to_use: Social simulation games (The Sims). RPG companion personality. When NPCs need persistent emotional state.
pitfalls: Over-engineering trap for most games. Emergent behavior is hard to predict and QA. Serialization complexity for save/load.

name: Procedural Animation State Machine
summary: Blend tree + IK for physics-responsive animation. State machine drives animation clips, IK adjusts limbs to terrain and interactions.
when_to_use: Characters on uneven terrain. Physics-based interactions. When canned animations look floaty or disconnected from the world.
pitfalls: Foot IK and hand IK are expensive. Weight curve tuning is painstaking. Blending artifacts when transitioning between procedural and canned.

name: AI Director (Dynamic Difficulty)
summary: Adaptive game intensity modulation. Monitor player stress metrics (health, ammo, time) and adjust spawns, item drops, and encounter density in real-time.
when_to_use: Horror games (Left 4 Dead model). Roguelikes with adaptive difficulty. Live service games with engagement optimization.
pitfalls: Difficulty whiplash if tuning is too aggressive. Requires extensive playtesting. Players may feel manipulated if too obvious.

name: Reputation / Faction System
summary: Track AI faction disposition toward the player using a reputation graph. Actions affect standing, which gates quests, prices, and NPC behavior.
when_to_use: Open-world RPGs with faction politics. Trading games. Games where player choices have social consequences.
pitfalls: Graph query cost at scale. Serialization complexity with many factions. Reputation decay balance is hard to tune.

name: Economy Loop
summary: Core game economy cycle: earn, spend, earn. Resources flow through sources (quests, loot) and sinks (upgrades, consumables) in a closed loop.
when_to_use: Any game with persistent currency or resources. Foundation for progression systems, crafting, and trading.
pitfalls: Inflation if sources outpace sinks. Economy breaking exploits. Balancing early-game scarcity vs late-game abundance.

name: Sink/Source Balance
summary: Maintain equilibrium between resource generation (sources) and resource consumption (sinks). Monitor flow rates to prevent inflation or deflation.
when_to_use: MMO economies, idle games, any live-service game with persistent progression. Economy health monitoring.
pitfalls: New content often adds sources without sinks. Player hoarding disrupts flow. Seasonal events can inject unexpected spikes.

name: Gear Treadmill
summary: Progression system where new gear obsoletes old gear, motivating continued play. Power level increases drive the loop.
when_to_use: Loot-driven ARPGs, MMOs with gear score, any game where equipment progression is the primary motivator.
pitfalls: Players feel effort is wasted when gear is obsoleted. Power creep makes old content trivial. Catch-up mechanics are hard.

name: Gacha / Loot Box
summary: Randomized reward system with tiered rarity. Players spend currency for random draws with visible probability tables and pity systems.
when_to_use: F2P mobile games, collectible systems, cosmetic monetization. Always with transparent rates and pity guarantees.
pitfalls: Legal restrictions in many jurisdictions. Player trust erosion without transparency. Pity system math is non-trivial. Ethical considerations.

name: Resource Conversion Chain
summary: Transform one resource type into another through crafting, refining, or trading. Chains create depth: ore to ingot to sword to enchanted sword.
when_to_use: Crafting systems, factory/automation games, survival games with resource processing.
pitfalls: Chain complexity overwhelms players. Bottleneck resources frustrate. UI for conversion paths needs careful design.

name: Auction House / Player Market
summary: Player-to-player trading system with listing, bidding, and buy-now. Price discovery through supply/demand. Transaction fees as an economy sink.
when_to_use: MMOs with player trading. Any multiplayer game where player-driven economy adds value.
pitfalls: Market manipulation by organized groups. Price crashes from duplication exploits. UI/UX for market browsing is complex.

name: Token Economy (In-Game Currency)
summary: Abstract game value into spendable tokens (gold, gems, credits). Multiple currency tiers gate different content and progression speeds.
when_to_use: Any game with purchasable content. F2P monetization. Reward systems.
pitfalls: Too many currencies confuse players. Exchange rates between currencies create exploits. Inflation monitoring needed.

name: Progression Gate
summary: Block access to content until a condition is met (level, item, quest completion). Controls pacing and ensures players are prepared.
when_to_use: Level-gated content, skill trees, story progression. Tutorial sequences. Difficulty curve management.
pitfalls: Gates that feel arbitrary frustrate players. Grinding-to-gate kills momentum. Must balance accessibility with challenge.

name: Soft/Hard Currency Split
summary: Two-tier currency: soft (earnable in-game, abundant) and hard (premium, scarce). Hard currency gates cosmetics or convenience, never power.
when_to_use: F2P games with ethical monetization. Any game separating earnable from purchasable rewards.
pitfalls: Pay-to-win perception if hard currency buys power. Conversion rate between currencies is exploitable. Regional pricing complexity.

name: Daily/Weekly Quest Loop
summary: Time-limited objectives that reset on a schedule. Drive daily engagement, provide structured goals, and gate premium rewards behind consistency.
when_to_use: Live service games, mobile games with retention goals, MMOs with daily content.
pitfalls: FOMO drives burnout. Quest fatigue if objectives are repetitive. Time zone handling complexity.

name: Continuous Collision Detection (CCD)
summary: Detect collisions between frames by sweeping shapes along their trajectory. Prevents fast-moving objects from tunneling through thin geometry.
when_to_use: Bullets, fast projectiles, racing games with thin walls. Any physics where discrete step misses collisions.
pitfalls: Significantly more expensive than discrete detection. Swept shape math is complex. CCD vs CCD (two fast objects) is very hard.

name: Swept Volume Test
summary: Compute the volume a shape occupies as it moves from A to B. Used for continuous collision, motion planning, and visibility checks.
when_to_use: CCD implementation detail. Motion planning for AI. Swept sphere/capsule tests for character controllers.
pitfalls: Rotational sweep is much harder than translational. Approximations (capsule sweep) sacrifice accuracy. GJK-based swept tests are complex.

name: Constraint Solver
summary: Iteratively resolve physics constraints (joints, contacts, limits) using sequential impulse or projected Gauss-Seidel. Core of rigid body physics.
when_to_use: Custom physics engines. Understanding engine constraint behavior. Ragdoll stability tuning.
pitfalls: Iteration count vs accuracy tradeoff. Constraint ordering affects convergence. Stacking instability with insufficient iterations.

name: Ragdoll / Physical Animation
summary: Transition from animated to physics-driven skeleton on death/hit. Constrained rigid bodies per bone with joint limits simulate realistic collapse.
when_to_use: Death animations, impact reactions, physics-based gameplay. Dynamic hit reactions.
pitfalls: Ragdoll jitter on contact. Joint limits need careful tuning per character. Blending from animation to ragdoll produces artifacts.

name: Buoyancy Simulation
summary: Apply upward force proportional to submerged volume. Sample water surface height at multiple points on the hull for realistic floating behavior.
when_to_use: Boat/ship physics, floating objects, water gameplay. Fishing games, naval combat, flood simulations.
pitfalls: Multi-point sampling is expensive. Wave interaction requires surface normal queries. Stability at rest needs damping.

name: Verlet Integration (Cloth/Softbody)
summary: Position-based physics integration where velocity is implicit from position history. Natural for cloth, rope, and softbody simulation with constraint satisfaction.
when_to_use: Cloth simulation, rope physics, hair, tentacles. When Euler integration produces instability.
pitfalls: Variable timestep breaks simulation. Constraint iteration count affects stiffness. Collision with complex geometry is expensive.

name: Deterministic Physics (Fixed-Point)
summary: Use fixed-point arithmetic and deterministic update ordering to guarantee identical simulation results across machines. Required for lockstep multiplayer.
when_to_use: Lockstep multiplayer (fighting games, RTS). Replay systems that must reproduce exactly. Competitive games requiring fairness.
pitfalls: Floating-point non-determinism across platforms. Fixed-point math is slower and less precise. Third-party physics engines are rarely deterministic.

name: Model-View-Presenter for Game UI
summary: Separate UI logic (Presenter) from display (View) and data (Model). Presenter mediates all interactions, making UI testable and swappable.
when_to_use: Complex game menus with business logic. Inventory screens, shop interfaces, character sheets with validation.
pitfalls: Over-engineering for simple HUD elements. Presenter proliferation in large UIs. Event routing between presenters adds complexity.

name: MVVM for Game UI
summary: Model-View-ViewModel with data binding. ViewModel exposes observable properties that Views bind to automatically. Changes propagate without manual update calls.
when_to_use: Data-heavy UIs (inventory, crafting, skill trees). When manual UI refresh logic becomes unmanageable. Unity with UI Toolkit.
pitfalls: Binding systems have performance cost. Debugging data flow through bindings is harder than direct calls. Framework dependency.

name: Event-Driven UI
summary: UI elements subscribe to game events and update reactively. Decouples game logic from UI rendering completely.
when_to_use: HUD elements that react to gameplay (health bars, score counters). Notification systems. Achievement popups.
pitfalls: Event ordering issues cause flickering. Memory leaks from forgotten subscriptions. Event storms during complex state changes.

name: Screen Stack Manager
summary: Stack-based UI navigation where screens push/pop. Back button always pops. Manages transitions, input focus, and background dimming.
when_to_use: Menu systems with nested screens (settings, audio, volume). Mobile game UIs. Pause menus over gameplay.
pitfalls: Stack corruption from async operations. Input handling when multiple screens overlap. Memory from keeping screens alive on stack.

name: Localization System
summary: Externalize all player-facing text into key-value tables per locale. Support string formatting, pluralization, RTL languages, and font fallbacks.
when_to_use: Any game targeting multiple languages. Plan for localization from day one as retrofitting is extremely expensive.
pitfalls: String concatenation breaks in other languages (word order varies). Pluralization rules differ per language. UI layout breaks with longer translations.

name: Accessibility Mode
summary: Configurable accessibility features: colorblind modes, remappable controls, subtitle options, screen reader support.
when_to_use: Every game. Accessibility is not optional as it expands your audience and is legally required in some jurisdictions.
pitfalls: Retrofitting accessibility is very expensive. Colorblind filters must not break gameplay readability. Controller remapping with combo inputs is complex.

name: UI Skin/Theme System
summary: Externalize UI visual properties (colors, fonts, spacing, icons) into swappable theme files. Runtime theme switching without rebuilding UI.
when_to_use: Games with customizable UI. Dark/light mode. Accessibility high-contrast themes. Seasonal event skins.
pitfalls: Theme variable proliferation. Ensuring all UI elements properly reference theme variables. Performance of runtime theme changes.

name: HUD as Diegetic Element
summary: Display game information within the game world rather than overlaid (health on character back display, ammo counter on weapon, map as in-game object).
when_to_use: Immersive games (Dead Space, Metro). VR games where flat overlays break presence. Horror games minimizing UI.
pitfalls: Readability at distance. Camera angle dependency. Combining diegetic with traditional HUD for accessibility.

name: Dynamic Weather System
summary: Procedural weather transitions with gameplay effects. Rain reduces visibility, snow affects traction, wind alters projectiles.
when_to_use: Open-world games, racing games, survival games. When weather adds tactical depth.
pitfalls: Shader cost for weather effects. Gameplay impact balance. State persistence across save/load.

name: Day/Night Cycle
summary: Time-of-day system driving lighting, NPC schedules, spawn tables, and gameplay modifiers. Compressed real-time or event-driven progression.
when_to_use: Open-world RPGs, survival games, farming sims. When time-of-day creates meaningful gameplay variation.
pitfalls: Lighting transition artifacts. NPC schedule synchronization. Player frustration if important content is time-locked.

name: Quest Graph / Journal System
summary: Directed graph of quest states with prerequisites, branching outcomes, and tracking. Serializable quest journal with objective checklists.
when_to_use: RPGs with branching storylines. Open-world games with side quests. Any game with multi-step objectives.
pitfalls: Graph complexity explodes with branching. Save/load must serialize full quest state. Debugging quest progression bugs is painful.

name: Inventory System (Grid/Slot)
summary: Item storage with grid-based (Resident Evil 4) or slot-based (Diablo) layout. Supports stacking, sorting, drag-drop, and weight/capacity limits.
when_to_use: RPGs, survival games, loot-heavy games. When item management is a core gameplay mechanic.
pitfalls: Grid tetris frustrates players. Drag-drop on mobile needs alternative. Serialization with item modifiers is complex.

name: Damage Numbers / Combat Feedback
summary: Floating damage numbers, hit flash, screen shake, and particle effects that communicate combat impact. Juice that makes combat feel responsive.
when_to_use: Any game with combat. ARPGs, hack-and-slash, MMOs. When attacks need to feel impactful.
pitfalls: Visual noise at high damage frequency. Number readability with many simultaneous hits. Performance with particle overdraw.

name: Minimap / Radar System
summary: Render a top-down or radar-style minimap showing player position, objectives, enemies, and points of interest. Rotate with camera or fixed north-up.
when_to_use: Open-world games, shooters with spatial awareness needs, strategy games.
pitfalls: Render target performance cost. Fog of war integration. Revealing too much information reduces exploration value.

name: Genre Template: Platformer
summary: Core loop: Move, Jump, Collect, Checkpoint. Player traverses obstacle courses with precision movement. Key mechanics: coyote time, jump buffering, variable jump height, parallax scrolling, checkpoint saves.
when_to_use: Starting a 2D or 3D platformer. Use this template to identify the minimum viable mechanic set and loop structure.
pitfalls: Neglecting feel tuning (coyote time, input buffering). Camera that fights the player. Checkpoints too far apart causing frustration.

name: Genre Template: Turn-Based RPG
summary: Core loop: Encounter, Action Selection, Resolution, Reward. Players explore, trigger encounters, choose actions from a menu, resolve via turn order, gain XP/loot. Key patterns: Command pattern, State Machine, Economy Loop.
when_to_use: Building a JRPG, tactics game, or any turn-based combat system with progression.
pitfalls: Slow pacing without auto-battle option. Unbalanced economy making grinding mandatory. Turn order complexity confusing players.

name: Genre Template: Action RPG
summary: Core loop: Explore, Combat, Loot, Upgrade. Real-time combat with character progression and gear systems. Key patterns: Behavior Tree (AI), Inventory Grid, Progression Gate, Damage Numbers.
when_to_use: Building a Diablo-like, Souls-like, or any real-time RPG with loot and leveling.
pitfalls: Loot tables that feel unrewarding. AI enemies that are trivially kitable. Stat inflation making early content irrelevant.

name: Genre Template: Tower Defense
summary: Core loop: Place, Wave, Defend, Resource. Players place defensive structures along paths, enemies arrive in waves, resources earned from kills fund upgrades. Key patterns: Pathfinding A*, Object Pool, Economy Loop.
when_to_use: Building a tower defense, base defense, or lane-based strategy game.
pitfalls: Optimal strategies that trivialize all maps. Waves that feel identical. Late-game performance with hundreds of entities.

name: Genre Template: Roguelite
summary: Core loop: Run, Die, Upgrade (persistent), Retry. Each run is procedurally varied. Death resets the run but persistent upgrades carry over. Key patterns: Procedural generation seed, Gear Treadmill, Observer, Save State.
when_to_use: Building a roguelite or roguelike with meta-progression between runs.
pitfalls: Persistent upgrades that make skill irrelevant. Procedural generation that feels random rather than designed. Runs that are too long or too short.

name: Genre Template: Battle Royale
summary: Core loop: Spawn, Loot, Shrink, Last One Standing. Large player count, shrinking play area forces engagements. Key patterns: Interest Management, Delta Compression, Replication Priority, World Streaming.
when_to_use: Building a battle royale or large-scale last-man-standing multiplayer game.
pitfalls: Server tick rate too low for fair combat. Zone shrink speed that feels arbitrary. Early game boredom with late-game chaos.

name: Genre Template: Idle/Clicker
summary: Core loop: Click, Resource, Automation, Prestige. Players generate resources through clicks, automate production, prestige resets for multipliers. Key patterns: Resource Conversion Chain, Token Economy, Dirty Flag, Update Method.
when_to_use: Building an idle game, clicker, or incremental game with offline progress.
pitfalls: Number inflation without meaningful choices. Offline progress that removes engagement. Prestige timing that feels forced.

name: Genre Template: Fighting Game
summary: Core loop: Round, Input, Animation, Hitbox. Frame-precise combat with input reads, animation cancels, and hitbox/hurtbox collision. Key patterns: Rollback Netcode, Deterministic Lockstep, FSM (state per move), Command Buffer.
when_to_use: Building a fighting game, beat-em-up, or any frame-precise competitive combat game.
pitfalls: Input lag above 3 frames ruins feel. Hitboxes that don't match visuals. Netcode that doesn't use rollback for online play.

name: Genre Template: FPS/Shooter
summary: Core loop: Move, Aim, Shoot, Respawn. First-person combat with projectile/hitscan weapons, movement mechanics, and respawn cycles. Key patterns: Client-Side Prediction, Lag Compensation, Object Pool (projectiles).
when_to_use: Building an FPS, third-person shooter, or any real-time shooting game with multiplayer.
pitfalls: Client-side hit detection exploits. Weapon balance that collapses to one meta. Movement that feels floaty without proper acceleration curves.

name: Genre Template: Strategy (RTS)
summary: Core loop: Build, Train, Micro, Macro. Players manage economy, build structures, train units, and command armies in real-time. Key patterns: Flow Field Pathfinding, Squad AI, Influence Map, Economy Loop.
when_to_use: Building an RTS, base-builder, or real-time tactics game with unit management.
pitfalls: Pathfinding breaking with hundreds of units. APM requirements that alienate casual players. Deterministic simulation bugs in replays.

name: Feel Tuning
summary: Designer says: 'The jump doesn't feel right.' Coder implements: coyote time (grace period after leaving edge), jump buffering (queue jump before landing), variable jump height (hold vs tap), landing squash/stretch animation.
when_to_use: Any platformer or action game where movement feel is critical. Apply after basic mechanics work but before polish pass.
pitfalls: Coyote time too long feels like flying. Jump buffer too generous removes timing skill. Squash/stretch that conflicts with hitbox.

name: Game Feel (Juice)
summary: Designer says: 'Add juice to the game.' Coder implements: screen shake on impact, particle bursts on events, time scale dip (hitstop), audio stingers, camera punch, chromatic aberration flash.
when_to_use: During polish phase when core mechanics are solid but feedback feels flat.
pitfalls: Over-juicing obscures gameplay. Screen shake without options causes motion sickness. Audio stacking creates noise.

name: Difficulty Scaling
summary: Designer says: 'Make it harder over time.' Coder implements: AI Director (dynamic difficulty), curve-based parameter scaling, rubber banding (catch-up mechanics), adaptive spawn rates.
when_to_use: Games with variable session lengths or mixed skill audiences. Apply when fixed difficulty alienates one audience.
pitfalls: Invisible rubber banding feels unfair when noticed. Scaling that punishes skill. AI Director overreacting to single events.

name: Onboarding Tutorial
summary: Designer says: 'Players don't understand the game.' Coder implements: progressive disclosure (reveal mechanics gradually), contextual tooltips triggered by proximity/first use, optional hint system with cooldown.
when_to_use: Any game with more than 3 core mechanics. Apply early in playtesting phase when new players are confused.
pitfalls: Unskippable tutorials that frustrate returning players. Tooltip overload. Teaching mechanics outside the context they're used in.

name: Player Retention Hook
summary: Designer says: 'Players aren't coming back.' Coder implements: daily quests with reward tiers, streak rewards (consecutive day bonuses), cliffhanger session endings (tease next unlock), push notification triggers.
when_to_use: Live service games, mobile games, or any game where DAU/MAU is a key metric.
pitfalls: FOMO mechanics that create resentment. Streak systems that punish missed days. Notifications that cause uninstalls.

name: Pacing and Tension
summary: Designer says: 'The game needs more tension.' Coder implements: threat assessment system, audio cue layering (music intensity tracks danger), enemy density scaling, restricted resource availability in tense zones.
when_to_use: Horror games, survival games, any game where emotional pacing matters.
pitfalls: Constant tension leads to fatigue. Audio cues that telegraph too much. Difficulty spikes that break immersion.

name: Emergent Gameplay
summary: Designer says: 'Let players discover things.' Coder implements: simulation stack (fire spreads, water flows), systemic interactions (elements combine), observable world state that players can manipulate creatively.
when_to_use: Open-world games, sandbox games, immersive sims where player creativity is a goal.
pitfalls: Emergence that breaks progression. Interactions that are entertaining but impossible to balance. Performance cost of simulating everything.

name: Accessibility Design
summary: Designer says: 'More players should enjoy this.' Coder implements: colorblind modes (deuteranopia/protanopia/tritanopia filters), remappable controls, subtitle system with speaker labels, reduced motion option, cognitive load reduction (simplified UI mode).
when_to_use: Every game. Start accessibility work at the architecture level, not as a post-launch patch.
pitfalls: Colorblind mode that only changes one UI element. Subtitles without size/contrast options. Accessibility menu that is itself inaccessible.

name: Metagame Loop
summary: Designer says: 'What do players do between sessions?' Coder implements: persistent progression (account level, unlocks), social comparison (leaderboards, friend activity), cosmetic identity (skins, titles, profile customization).
when_to_use: Multiplayer games, live service games, any game that needs long-term engagement beyond core gameplay.
pitfalls: Pay-to-win progression that destroys competitive integrity. Leaderboards that discourage new players. Cosmetics that affect hitboxes.

name: Loot Design
summary: Designer says: 'Make drops feel rewarding.' Coder implements: weighted loot tables with rarity tiers, pity system (guaranteed drop after N misses), visual/audio escalation per rarity, smart loot (bias toward unused types).
when_to_use: Any game with item drops: RPGs, looter shooters, roguelites, gacha systems.
pitfalls: Pure RNG without pity feels punishing. Rarity inflation making common items worthless. Loot tables that players reverse-engineer and exploit.

name: Narrative Trigger
summary: Designer says: 'Story events should feel reactive.' Coder implements: Observer pattern for narrative events, event queue with priority, flag-based story state machine, bark system for contextual dialogue.
when_to_use: Story-driven games, open-world games with emergent narrative, any game where NPCs react to player actions.
pitfalls: Flag explosion with hundreds of boolean states. Events that fire out of order. Dialogue that references events the player hasn't seen.

name: Level Design Language
summary: Designer says: 'Players should know where to go.' Coder implements: lighting as guide (bright areas attract), color language (consistent color = consistent meaning), silhouette targets (distinctive landmark shapes), breadcrumb collectibles.
when_to_use: Any game with spatial navigation. Especially important when minimizing explicit UI markers.
pitfalls: Over-reliance on one technique. Guiding that removes exploration. Color language that conflicts with accessibility needs.

name: Feedback Loop Design
summary: Designer says: 'Player actions need clearer feedback.' Coder implements: damage numbers with scaling, hit stop (frame freeze on impact), screen space effects (vignette on low health), controller rumble patterns, UI flash on state change.
when_to_use: Any action game where the player needs immediate confirmation that their input had an effect.
pitfalls: Feedback so loud it obscures the game. Inconsistent feedback across similar actions. Accessibility issues with screen effects.

name: Social Presence
